one_line_takeaway: FSTab shows that LLM-generated web apps leak a compact, model-specific attack prior from purely visible UI features alone, letting a black-box adversary hit at least one real backend vulnerability class 69-88% of the time (vs 10-20% for a matched-budget random baseline) even when the target's application domain was never seen during construction.
key_finding: On WebGenBench held-out prompts, FSTab reaches domain-averaged ASR up to 85.00% (CodeQL) | 88.00% (Semgrep) for Claude-4.5 Opus and 84.00% | 86.00% for GPT-5.2 (Table 3); GPT-5.2 and Claude-4.5 Opus were the strongest models on held-out programs, and the paper reports perfect ASR/ACR in multiple held-out domains in the full per-domain appendix table. Under cross-domain transfer (target domain fully excluded from construction), Claude-4.5 Opus retains 78.93% | 85.67% ASR and 78.93% | 84.97% ACR, and GPT-5.2 retains 80.92% | 85.42% ASR -- explicitly cited by the authors as evidence the learned priors are not merely memorizing one application class. Against the matched-budget Random-Budget baseline on WebGenBench held-out, FSTab achieves 69.4% vs 10.1% ASR under CodeQL and 72.5% vs 20.3% ASR under Semgrep (3.6x-6.9x advantage). After removing the top-3 most frequent training rules (to rule out FSTab merely exploiting dominant-rule frequency), FSTab retains 63.6% ASR / 52.7% ACR on rare-rule projects under Semgrep versus 18.3% ASR / 6.9% ACR for Random-Budget (3.5x-7.6x improvement); under CodeQL top-5 exclusion the ratio reaches 6.2x ASR / 7.6x ACR. Precision (APR) is markedly lower than ASR by design: Gemini-3 Pro attains the highest model-level APR at 54.4%, followed by GPT-5.2 at 36.2%. The central recurrence finding (Table 4, Figure 3): cross-domain transfer (CDT) exceeds within-domain recurrence (DVR) for every one of the six models, by roughly 18 points on average (e.g., Gemini-3 Pro: CDT=58.67% vs DVR=41.39%; Composer: CDT=57.32% vs DVR=46.43%), which the authors interpret as evidence that recurring vulnerabilities reflect stable model-level coding habits rather than domain-local artifacts. The mismatched-fingerprint stress test shows ACR drops by 16.4 points on average (1.36x degradation) when querying with the wrong model's table, with per-model ASR losses reaching 25.7 points for Grok, 14.3 for Composer, and 13.8 for Claude-4.5 Opus -- 4-10x larger than per-seed standard deviation, supporting that the learned tables are genuinely model-specific fingerprints rather than a shared generic prior. FSTab is also compact: under the minimum-support filter it retains only 28 CodeQL rules and 39 Semgrep rules (versus 300+/5,000+ total rules in those scanners), and the deduplicated prediction set averages 4.86 CodeQL and 8.03 Semgrep rules per project, keeping manual validation tractable. A construction-budget ablation (Appendix D.4.1) shows ASR/ACR plateau around only 20 construction projects.
core_contribution: The paper introduces FSTab (Feature-Security Table), a model-specific, black-box lookup table that maps observable frontend UI features (from a 59-action taxonomy: login, upload, checkout, etc.) of an LLM-generated web application to a ranked list of likely hidden backend vulnerability rules. FSTab is built offline by an attacker who generates their own applications with the same target model family, labels backend vulnerabilities with CodeQL and Semgrep, and aggregates feature-rule co-occurrence using a PMI-style association score with a diversity penalty (Eq. 2-3, alpha=0.5, lambda=0.8, k=25, min-support C(f,r)>=3). At attack time, the adversary only observes a deployed victim application's public UI/DOM/network behavior, maps it to the same feature taxonomy, and unions the table's predicted rule sets (Eq. 4) -- no source code access to the victim is required. The paper's own contribution (as stated in Section 1: 'Our contributions are threefold') is (1) the FSTab black-box lookup attack itself, (2) a recurrence-measurement framework across four axes (FVR, RVP, DVR, CDT), and (3) an empirical finding that cross-domain transfer (CDT) exceeds within-domain recurrence (DVR) for every evaluated model -- the 'universality gap' -- implying recurring vulnerabilities reflect stable model-level coding habits rather than isolated prompt or domain artifacts.
threat_model: Two-stage black-box adversary, explicitly scoped by the authors (Section 3, 'Threat model'). Stage 1 (offline, attacker-controlled): the attacker queries the same publicly accessible model family used by the victim, generates their own applications, and builds FSTab once using source-level static analysis (CodeQL/Semgrep) on their own generations -- this construction step uses source access but only on attacker-owned code, and the resulting table is reusable across many victims. Stage 2 (online, victim-directed): the attacker observes only the deployed target application's public interface (UI, DOM, network-visible behavior), maps observed actions to the 59-action taxonomy, and queries the precomputed table -- no source-code access, provider-side internals, or model weights of the victim are required. The attacker must identify (or fingerprint) the model family that generated the victim application. The authors explicitly caveat that FSTab predicts likely scanner-detected vulnerability classes for prioritization, not certified end-to-end exploits; a defender/attacker must still validate reachability manually or dynamically.
limitations: Stated by the authors in Section 5: (1) FSTab assumes either known model identity or a reliable fingerprinting step to select the correct table for a victim application. (2) It depends on correctly identifying the target's observable UI actions; in the paper's own experiments these are canonicalized via automated source-level extraction on attacker-owned generations for consistency/reproducibility, which the authors explicitly flag as an evaluation convenience, not proof that manual/UI-based extraction at deployment time works identically (they only performed a qualitative manual sanity check, not a full quantitative victim-side re-derivation). (3) The evaluation is restricted to LLM-generated web applications (WebGenBench, E2EDev), where the observable feature space is unusually rich compared to smaller script-generation tasks; generalization to non-web or agent-generated repositories/mobile apps is untested (flagged as future work). (4) FSTab predicts likely scanner-detected vulnerability classes, not confirmed end-to-end exploits -- static analysis is only a proxy for exploitability, and the reported 82%/74% (CodeQL/Semgrep) manual-audit precision figures mean roughly a fifth to a quarter of underlying scanner labels used as ground truth may themselves be inaccurate. (5) Precision (APR) is low by construction (best model-level APR only 54.4%, Gemini-3 Pro), meaning most individual predicted rules in a project are not confirmed correct even when at least one hit occurs (ASR). Not addressed by the authors: no external/independent evaluation of the manual audit; no analysis of cost/scalability if the taxonomy needs to generalize beyond the 59 predefined actions; the paper's own reported experiment cost was $419.40 using Cursor Ultra across 900 constructed and tested applications, indicating the offline construction stage is nontrivial in practitioner cost despite plateauing around 20 projects per model.

datasets_used: WebGenBench (Lu et al., 2026) - 900 generated programs across 5 domains: E-commerce, Internal Tools, Social Media, Blogging, Dashboards
E2EDev (Liu et al., 2025) - 150 generated programs, supplementary cross-benchmark transfer
CodeQL 2.23.8 and Semgrep 1.147.0 static analysis findings used as ground-truth vulnerability labels
quantitative_results: WebGenBench held-out ASR: Claude-4.5 Opus 85.00%|88.00% (CodeQL|Semgrep), GPT-5.2 84.00%|86.00%, Gemini-3 Pro 76.00%|84.00%, Composer 78.33%|82.00%, Grok 76.67%|73.00%, Gemini-3 Flash 60.00%|75.33% (Table 3)
Cross-domain ASR: GPT-5.2 80.92%|85.42%, Claude-4.5 Opus 78.93%|85.67%, Gemini-3 Pro 71.31%|81.00% (Table 3)
FSTab vs Random-Budget baseline, WebGenBench held-out: ASR 69.4% vs 10.1% (CodeQL, 6.9x), 72.5% vs 20.3% (Semgrep, 3.6x)
After top-3 rule exclusion (Semgrep, rare-rule projects): FSTab 63.6% ASR / 52.7% ACR vs Random-Budget 18.3% ASR / 6.9% ACR (3.5x-7.6x)
After top-5 rule exclusion (CodeQL): FSTab/Random-Budget ratio 6.2x ASR, 7.6x ACR
Model-level APR (precision): Gemini-3 Pro highest at 54.4%, GPT-5.2 second at 36.2%
Recurrence scores (Table 4): FVR highest for Gemini-3 Pro (51.23%); RVP and DVR highest for Composer (35.53%, 46.43%); CDT highest for Gemini-3 Pro (58.67%)
Universality gap: CDT exceeds DVR for all 6 models, by ~18 points on average (Figure 3)
Mismatched-fingerprint stress test: ACR drops 16.4 points on average (1.36x degradation); per-model ASR losses up to 25.7 points (Grok), 14.3 (Composer), 13.8 (Claude-4.5 Opus)
Rule-space compression: FSTab retains only 28 CodeQL and 39 Semgrep rules (from 300+ and 5,000+ available), average query returns 4.86 CodeQL / 8.03 Semgrep rules per project
Construction-budget ablation: ASR/ACR plateau around 20 construction projects
8.6 extracted UI features per project on average across WebGenBench
Total experiment financial cost: $419.40 (Cursor Ultra subscription + usage overage) across 900 generated applications
baselines_compared: Random-Budget baseline: predicts the same number of rules per project as FSTab, sampled uniformly from the learnable rule space (used to isolate FSTab's genuine predictive signal from mere prediction-set-size effects)

Mismatched-fingerprint (wrong-model-profile) baseline: FSTab queried with a different model's table, sampled uniformly from the other five models, averaged over ten seeds (used to test whether learned tables are genuinely model-specific)
